name: Amsterdam Pride
description: Pride Amsterdam or Amsterdam Pride, also formerly known as the Amsterdam Gay Pride, is a citywide queer-festival held annually at the center of Amsterdam, Netherlands during the first weekend of August. Pride Amsterdam was first organized in 1996, meant as a festival to celebrate freedom and diversity. It was therefore not like many other Gay Prides, which began as demonstrations for equal rights. name: Andong Mask Dance Festival
bestFor: Cultural immersion; Traditional heritage; Arts and design
description: The Andong International Mask Dance Festival is Korea's premier showcase of the hahoetal mask dance tradition — satirical masked performances that date to the Joseon Dynasty. Teams from Korea and over 20 countries perform traditional mask dances from their own cultures, creating a genuine world festival of a specific art form. Andong's Hahoe Folk Village (UNESCO-listed) provides the most atmospheric backdrop. The performances use exaggerated masks to satirise aristocracy, clergy, and social hierarchy — subversive humour encoded in performance.
travelNotes: Andong is 2.5 hours from Seoul by KTX/bus. Late September in Korea is ideal weather — cool, clear, and colourful with autumn foliage beginning. Hahoe Village performances are in an outdoor theatre by the river. Festival runs over 10 days; the opening weekend is the most packed. The village itself is worth a full day independent of the festival.

name: Athens Epidaurus Festival
bestFor: Cultural immersion; Summer travel; Traditional heritage
description: Athens – Epidaurus Festival is an annual arts festival that takes place in Athens and Epidaurus, from May to October. It is one of the most famous festivals in Greece. It is held every year during the summer months (Fridays and Saturdays in July and August), in part in the ancient theatre of Epidaurus, on the archaeological site of the Asclepion. The festival includes musical, theatrical and other cultural events. They include performances of tragedies by Aeschylus, Sophocles and Euripides, as well as surviving comedies by Aristophanes; some performances consist simply of revivals of ancient myths or contemporary plays written on the basis of ancient dramas.

name: Ati-Atihan Festival
description: Ati-Atihan in Kalibo, Aklan is the Philippines' most riotous festival and a direct ancestor of Rio-style street carnival. Participants cover their faces and bodies in soot or black paint in homage to the indigenous Ati people, then dance through the streets for days in elaborate costumes to thunderous tribal drumming. The festival honours the Santo Niño (child Jesus) but the African-inspired energy and visual intensity are unlike anything else in the Philippines. Called 'the mother of all Philippine festivals'.
travelNotes: Kalibo has its own airport with connections from Manila and Cebu. January is dry season in the Visayas — ideal weather. The main street dancing is on the third Sunday of January. The festival is community-led and intimate compared to Sinulog in Cebu; more authentic, less polished. Book Kalibo accommodation well ahead — the town is small.

name: Awa Odori
description: Awa Odori is Japan's largest dance festival, held annually in Tokushima during Obon season. Over 1.3 million spectators watch 100,000 performers dance through the city in coordinated troupes called ren. The chant is famous across Japan: 'The dancing fool and the watching fool are both fools — so why not dance?' Performers wear traditional straw hats and yukata, moving in a distinctive two-beat rhythm through the city's main arteries. The festival traces its origins to 1586 and has been unbroken for over 400 years.
travelNotes: Book accommodation 3–4 months ahead — Tokushima fills completely. The main paid viewing stands sell out fast but free street viewing is excellent. Trains from Osaka take about 2 hours via the Mugi Line. Evening sessions (6pm–10pm) are the most atmospheric. Comfortable walking shoes essential — you'll cover a lot of ground following different ren troupes.

name: Baalbek International Festival
bestFor: Arts and design; Cultural immersion; Traditional heritage
description: Baalbek International Festival stages classical music, opera, ballet, and world music concerts inside the 2,000-year-old Roman temple complex of Baalbek — the most intact Roman ruins in the Middle East. The Temple of Bacchus serves as backstage; the Great Court of the Temple of Jupiter becomes the main stage. Artists including Fairouz, Ella Fitzgerald, Herbert von Karajan, and Miles Davis have performed here. Check Lebanon travel advisories before planning.
travelNotes: Lebanon's political and security situation requires monitoring before booking. The festival runs July–August. Baalbek is 85km from Beirut in the Bekaa Valley, about 1.5 hours by road. The ruins themselves (one of Lebanon's two UNESCO sites) justify the visit regardless of the festival programme. July evenings at Baalbek are warm and the ruins are illuminated.

name: Bali Arts Festival
bestFor: Cultural immersion; Traditional heritage; Arts and design
description: The Bali Arts Festival (Pesta Kesenian Bali) is a month-long celebration of Balinese performing arts held at the Taman Werdhi Budaya Arts Centre in Denpasar. Every district of Bali sends its finest dancers, musicians, and craftspeople to compete and perform. Kecak fire dance, Legong, Barong, shadow puppetry, gamelan orchestras, and traditional weaving demonstrations run daily. The opening procession alone — a kilometres-long parade of regionally costumed performers — is one of the most spectacular in Asia.
travelNotes: June–July is dry season in Bali — ideal conditions. The Arts Centre is in Denpasar rather than the tourist hub of Seminyak/Ubud; a 30–45 minute drive. Most performances are free or very low cost. The opening ceremony parade on the first Saturday is the must-see event — arrive early for position. Evening kecak performances under the stars are particularly atmospheric.

name: Barranquilla Carnival
description: The Barranquilla Carnival (Spanish: Carnaval de Barranquilla) is one of Colombia's most important folkloric celebrations, and one of the biggest carnivals in the world. The carnival has traditions that date back to the 19th century. Four days before Lent, Barranquilla decks itself out to receive national and foreign tourists to join together with the city's inhabitants to enjoy four days of intense festivities. During the carnival, Barranquilla's normal activities are put aside as the city gets busy with street dances, musical and masquerade parades. The Carnival Of Barranquilla includes dances such as the Spanish paloteo, African Congo, and indigenous mice y mica's. Many styles of Colombian music are also performed, most prominently cumbia, and instruments include drums and wind ensembles.…

name: Bastille Day — Paris
description: Bastille Day is the common name given in English-speaking countries to the national day of France, which is celebrated on 14 July each year. It is referred to, both legally and commonly, as le 14 juillet (French: [lə katɔʁz(ə) ʒɥijɛ]) in French, though la fête nationale is also used in the press. French National Day is the anniversary of the Storming of the Bastille on 14 July 1789, a major event of the French Revolution, as well as the Fête de la Fédération that celebrated the unity of the French people on 14 July 1790. Celebrations are held throughout France. One that has been reported as "the oldest and largest military parade in Europe" is held on 14 July on the Champs-Élysées in Paris in front of the President of France, along with other French officials and foreign guests.

name: Belgrade Beer Fest
description: The Belgrade Beer Fest (Serbian: Београдски фестивал пива / Beogradski festival piva) is an annual festival of beer in Belgrade, Serbia. Started in 2003, the festival is held annually over five days each August as a showcase event for various beer producers. In addition to domestic and foreign brews, the festival features live music performances each evening. It has quickly grown in size and popularity: in 2004, it attracted over 75,000 foreign visitors to Belgrade, and in 2005, it was the second most visited festival in Serbia with 300,000 visitors. In 2009, it attracted more than 650,000 visitors, and in 2010, the festival attracted about 900,000 visitors. No festival was held in 2020. The festival entrance was free until 2023.

description: The Beltane Fire Society is a charity run by a board of volunteers, who oversee the year-round operation of the organisation responsible for the Beltane Fire Festival. The society is funded entirely by donations through its membership, from Beltane Fire Festival ticket sales, and from merchandising. It receives no donations from any public funds. The Beltane Fire Society organises the Beltane Fire Festival on Calton Hill and the Samhuinn Fire Festival on the Royal Mile, both in Edinburgh, Scotland. The society was formed in 1988 for the second Beltane Fire Festival (the first on Calton Hill). The Beltane Fire Society also organises events for the other Celtic cross-quarter days, i.e., Imbolc and Lughnasadh and Samhuinn.

bestFor: Food travel; Group trips; Traditional heritage
description: The Bergkirchweih is an annual Volksfest (beer festival and travelling funfair) in Erlangen, Germany. Locals nickname it Berch, which is the East Franconian pronunciation of the German word Berg, meaning mountain or hill. The Bergkirchweih starts on the Thursday before Pentecost at 5 PM. The opening ceremony, called "Anstich," is performed by the city's mayor and takes place each year at a different beer cellar, which traditionally serves as the storage and cooling facility for local breweries. Thousands of people gather to witness this inaugural event, eagerly awaiting the opportunity to receive a stein of free beer from the first barrel. According to tradition, twelve days later, the last beer barrel is ceremonially buried at the cellar where the next Anstich will occur.…

name: Birkirkara St Helen Feast
description: St Helen's Basilica (Maltese: Il-Bażilika ta' Sant' Elena) is an 18th-century Roman Catholic church situated in Birkirkara, Malta. It is built on the baroque design by Salvu Borg and built by Mason Domenico Cachia. The design was inspired by, and is similar to, the Mdina Cathedral by Lorenzo Gafa. It was built to replace the role of the main old Parish Church of St. Mary (still parish church of the area) that was damaged in the 1856 earthquake. It eventually became the Collegiate Church and then a Minor Basilica. The current parish archpriest is Mons Louis Suban.

name: Boryeong Mud Festival
bestFor: Adrenaline seekers; Group trips; Summer travel
description: Boryeong Mud Festival started in 1998 as a promotion for the local cosmetics industry and has become one of Korea's most popular summer events. The mud — drawn from Boryeong's tidal flats and supposedly rich in minerals — fills massive inflatable pools, slides, and wrestling arenas on Daecheon Beach. Tens of thousands of visitors (including a large international crowd) spend days covered head to toe in grey mud, competing in mud-based contests and attending beach concerts. Unapologetically silly and genuinely fun.
travelNotes: Boryeong is 2 hours from Seoul by express bus or train to Daecheon station. July/August in Korea is hot and humid — the mud and beach are appropriate. The festival provides changing facilities and showers. Accommodation near Daecheon Beach books out fast; reserve at least a month ahead. The international crowd is large; English signage is good.

name: Braderie de Lille
description: The Lille Braderie (French: Braderie de Lille) is a braderie, or annual street market/flea market, that takes place on the weekend of the first Sunday of September in Lille, France, in the northern Hauts-de-France region. It dates back to the 12th century, attracting nearly three million visitors each year. The Braderie de Lille is one of the largest gatherings in France and the largest flea market in Europe. In 2014 the Braderie was reported to host 100 km of market stalls to over 10,000 exhibitors. During the Braderie, the city hosts a music festival. The traditional food eaten during the festival, moules-frites, typically results in built-up heaps of mussel shells around the city. In 2016, the Braderie was cancelled by Lille mayor Martine Aubry, following terrorist incidents in France.

name: Burning Man
bestFor: Group trips; Art and design; Adrenaline seekers
description: Burning Man is a week-long large-scale desert event focused on "community, art, self-expression, and self-reliance" held annually in the Western United States. The event's name comes from its ceremony on the second to last night of the event: the symbolic burning of a large wooden effigy, referred to as the Man, the Saturday evening before Labor Day. Since 1990, the event has been at Black Rock City in northwestern Nevada, a temporary city erected in the Black Rock Desert about 100 miles (160 km) north-northeast of Reno. According to Burning Man co-founder Larry Harvey in 2004, the event is guided by ten stated principles: radical inclusion, gifting, decommodification, radical self-reliance, radical self-expression, communal effort, civic responsibility, leaving no trace, participation, and immediacy.…

description: The busójárás (Hungarian, meaning "Busó-walking"; in Croatian: Pohod bušara) is an annual celebration of the Šokci living in the town of Mohács, Hungary, held at the end of the Carnival season ("farsang"), ending the day before Ash Wednesday. The celebration features Busós (people wearing traditional masks) and includes folk music, masquerading, parades and dancing. Busójárás lasts six days, usually during February. It starts on a Thursday, followed by the Kisfarsang (Little Farsang) carnival on Friday, with the biggest celebration, farsang vasárnap (Farsang Sunday) on the Sunday before Easter Sunday; the celebration then ends with farsangtemetés (Burial of Farsang) on the following Tuesday (Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras). These traditional festivities have been inscribed on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity of the UNESCO in 2009.…

Customary party accessories are novelty paper hats, paper tablecloths, paper lanterns (often depicting the Man in the Moon), and bibs. Many events take place in central Kyoto and at the Yasaka Shrine, the festival's patron shrine, located in Kyoto's famous Gion district, which gives the festival its name. It is formally a Shinto festival, and its original purposes were purification and pacification of disease-causing entities. There are many ceremonies held during the festival, but it is best known for its two Yamaboko Junkō (山鉾巡行) processions of floats, which take place on July 17 and 24. The three nights leading up to each day of a procession are sequentially called yoiyoiyoiyama (宵々々山), yoiyoiyama (宵々山), and yoiyama (宵山).…

name: Gion Matsuri Yamaboko Junko
description: The Yamaboko Junko processions are the centrepiece of Kyoto's month-long Gion Matsuri — Japan's most famous festival. On July 17 and 24, enormous wooden floats (some 25 metres tall and weighing 12 tonnes) are pulled through the city centre by teams of men in traditional dress. The floats carry musicians playing the haunting Gion bayashi music and are decorated with Nishijin-ori tapestries, some dating to the 16th century. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. The evenings before each procession (Yoiyama) are street festivals with stalls and open float viewing.

Hunters in elaborate traditional Kazakh costumes ride on horseback across open steppe while their eagles perform hunting demonstrations and speed competitions. name: Guy Fawkes Night
description: Guy Fawkes Night, also known as Guy Fawkes Day, Bonfire Night and Fireworks Night, is an annual commemoration observed on 5 November, primarily in Great Britain, involving bonfires and fireworks displays. Its history begins with the events of 5 November 1605 O.S., when Guy Fawkes, a member of the Gunpowder Plot, was arrested while guarding explosives the plotters had placed beneath the House of Lords. The Catholic plotters had intended to assassinate Protestant King James I and his parliament. Celebrating that the king had survived, people lit bonfires around London. Months later, the Observance of 5th November Act 1605 (3 Jas. 1. c. 1) mandated an annual public day of thanksgiving for the plot's failure. Within a few decades Gunpowder Treason Day, as it was known, became the predominant English state commemoration.…

description: Hadaka Matsuri (Naked Festival) at Saidaiji Temple in Okayama is one of Japan's strangest and most compelling events. Roughly 10,000 men wearing only white loincloths (fundoshi) in near-freezing temperatures compete to catch sacred wooden sticks (shingi) thrown by a priest at midnight. The belief is that whoever catches them will have a year of good fortune. The crush of bodies in the darkness is intense and genuinely chaotic. It has been held for over 500 years.
travelNotes: The festival runs from around 10pm with the main event at midnight. Dress extremely warmly as a spectator — standing outside in February in Okayama is cold. Participants are assigned entry by lottery. The atmosphere is unlike anything else in Japan: incense, chanting, and 10,000 near-naked men surging through the dark. Take the JR Ako Line to Saidaiji Station.

The setting alone — crumbling gopurams, giant boulders, banana plantations, and the Tungabhadra river — makes Hampi one of the most atmospheric festival locations in Asia. Devised by Norman, Rhoda and Peter Florence in 1988, the festival was described by Bill Clinton in 2001 as "The Woodstock of the mind". Tony Benn said: "In my mind it's replaced Christmas". It has become a prominent festival in British culture, and sessions at the festival have been recorded for television and radio programmes such as The Readers' and Writers' Roadshow and The One Show. description: Hogmanay ( HOG-mə-nay, -⁠NAY, Scots: [ˌhɔɡməˈneː]) is the Scots word for the last day of the old year and is synonymous with the celebration of the New Year in the Scottish manner. It is normally followed by further celebration on the morning of New Year's Day (1 January) and, in some cases, 2 January—a Scottish bank holiday. In a few contexts, the word Hogmanay is used more loosely to describe the entire period consisting of the last few days of the old year and the first few days of the new year. For instance, not all events held under the banner of Edinburgh's Hogmanay take place on 31st of December. Customs vary throughout Scotland and usually include gift-giving and visiting the homes of friends and neighbours, with particular attention given to the first-foot, the first guest of the new year.

Holi originated in ancient Indian subcontinent and is predominantly celebrated in the Indian subcontinent, but has also spread to other regions of Asia and parts of the Western world. Holi also celebrates the arrival of spring in India and Nepal, the end of winter, and the blossoming of love. It is also an invocation for a good spring harvest season. It lasts for a night and a day, starting on the evening of the Purnima (full moon day) falling on the Hindu calendar month of Phalguna, which falls around the middle of March in the Gregorian calendar.

It begins with the commemoration of Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday, marks the betrayal of Jesus on Spy Wednesday (Holy Wednesday), climaxing with the commemoration of the Last Supper on Maundy Thursday (Holy Thursday) and the Passion of Jesus on Good Friday (Holy Friday). Holy Week concludes with Christ's death and descent into hell on Holy Saturday. For all Christian traditions, it is a moveable observance. In Eastern Christianity, which also calls it Great Week, it is the week following Great Lent and Lazarus Saturday, starting on the evening of Palm Sunday and concluding on the evening of Great Saturday.…

name: Hungry Ghost Festival

In Penang — which has the most elaborate Hungry Ghost Festival outside of Taiwan — entire streets burn enormous quantities of joss paper, incense, and elaborate paper offerings. Community stages (getai) host Hokkien opera and live entertainment for the ghosts. Walking through Penang's streets at night during the festival, with braziers burning and opera playing to empty seats reserved for spirits, is genuinely otherworldly.

It is a profoundly Korean winter experience — locals bundled against the cold, grilling fish over gas burners on frozen ice, children on sleds. Around 1 million visitors attend over the festival's run.
travelNotes: Hwacheon is 2.5 hours from Seoul by bus — no direct train. January temperatures average -10°C; dress in full winter gear. Fishing rods provided on-site. The fish are astonishingly good grilled fresh on the ice. Weekend crowds are large; midweek is more relaxed. The festival village has food stalls, equipment hire, and heated rest tents.

Professional kite flyers from countries including China, Japan, France, USA, and across India participate with enormous display kites, fighter kites, and novelty designs. name: Isle of Man TT

description: The Isle of Man TT (Tourist Trophy; sometimes TT) races are an annual motorcycle racing event held on the Isle of Man in May and June of mostly every year since its inaugural race in 1907. The two-week event is sanctioned by the Auto-Cycle Union, which also organises the event. The Manx government owns the rights to and promotes the event. The Isle of Man TT is a series of time-trial format races, run on public roads closed for racing. The event consists of one week of practice and qualifying sessions, followed by one week of racing. The closed public roads form the Snaefell Mountain Course, a 37.73-mile (60.72 km) route containing 219 turns that traverses through a mix of urban and rural areas. In folk tradition, it was revered as the day of the summer solstice and was originally celebrated on the shortest night of the year, which is on 21-22 or 23-24 of June in Czechia, Poland, Slovakia, Bulgaria (where it is called Enyovden), and was adopted in Ukraine since 2023. name: Jallikattu Bull Taming Festival
bestFor: Cultural immersion; Adrenaline seekers; Traditional heritage
description: Jallikattu is the ancient Tamil bull-taming sport of the Pongal harvest festival — a practice documented for over 2,500 years. Zebu bulls are released from a pen and participants must grab and hold the bull's hump for a prescribed distance to win. The bulls are bred for aggression and the event is genuinely dangerous; injuries to both participants and spectators are common. It was banned in 2017, reinstated after mass protests, and remains a symbol of Tamil cultural identity. The Alanganallur version near Madurai is the most famous.
travelNotes: Jallikattu takes place on Mattu Pongal — the third day of Pongal (Tamil harvest festival), usually January 15. Madurai has an airport and good rail connections from Chennai. The intensity and speed of the event is extraordinary.

During the tournament, also the Kırkpınar Festival is organized. Oil wrestling is a sport at the forefront of traditional Turkish sports. Wrestling was held in all of the fairs organized all over the Ottoman Empire, but the wrestler who won the "chief" title only in Kırkpınar was known as the "chief wrestler" until the next year's Kırkpınar wrestling. In this tradition, Saint Nicholas rewards well-behaved children with small gifts, while Krampus punishes badly behaved ones with birch rods. Held at the confluence of the Ganges, Yamuna, and mythical Saraswati rivers, pilgrims come to bathe on auspicious dates (Shahi Snan) believing it will cleanse sins and break the cycle of rebirth. In 2016 proclaimed as the 7th largest carnival in the world by Lonely Planet. Its main figure, known as Kurent or Korent, has been popularly (but incorrectly) reinterpreted as an extravagant god of unrestrained pleasure and hedonism in early Slavic customs. In today's festival, groups of kurents or kurenti wear traditional sheepskin garments while holding wooden clubs with hedgehog skins attached called ježevke, the noise of which is believed to "chase away winter".…

description: Lathmar Holi in Barsana (the village of Radha, consort of Krishna) is perhaps the most extraordinary single day in the Hindu festival calendar. Women from Barsana chase men from the neighbouring village of Nandgaon with wooden sticks (lathis), who must shield themselves while trying to apply colour. The ritual re-enacts the mythology of Krishna teasing Radha. The atmosphere — a dense crowd, clouds of coloured powder, bamboo sticks flying, dhol drums, and devotional singing — is overwhelming in the best way. One day only; one location.

Lewes is part of the Sussex bonfire tradition. There are seven societies putting on six separate processions and firework displays throughout Lewes on 5 November. As well as this, 25–30 societies from all around Sussex come to Lewes on the fifth to march the streets.…

name: Lopburi Monkey Banquet Festival
description: The Lopburi Monkey Banquet is exactly what it sounds like — a feast prepared for the 3,000 long-tailed macaques that inhabit the ancient Khmer ruins of Phra Prang Sam Yot in the centre of Lopburi. Two tonnes of fruit, vegetables, and prepared food are laid out on decorated tables beside the ruins as an annual thank-you to the monkeys (who bring tourists and thereby prosperity). The monkeys climb over everything and everyone within reach. The event was started in 1989 by a local hotel owner and has become Lopburi's most famous annual event.
travelNotes: Lopburi is 150km north of Bangkok — 2 hours by train. Late November. The monkeys are present year-round; the buffet is the last Sunday of November. Keep bags, glasses, and loose items secure — the monkeys are bold thieves. The event is free. The Khmer ruins of Lopburi are genuinely significant historically; the monkey dimension is the bonus.

name: Losar Tibetan New Year

The Namgyal Monastery at the Dalai Lama's temple complex hosts elaborate ceremonies; traditional thangka scroll paintings are displayed; monasteries whitewash their walls and prepare elaborate torma (ritual dough sculptures). The exile Tibetan community's celebration of Losar has an undercurrent of cultural preservation that makes it particularly moving.
travelNotes: McLeod Ganj is 90km from Pathankot (nearest railhead) — about 3 hours by road. February in Dharamsala is cold (5–10°C) with possible snow. name: Loy Krathong

Thousands of candlelit krathong (lotus-shaped floats) are released onto the moats and lakes surrounding the 13th-century temples, while fire performances, traditional dance, and illuminated ruins create a setting of extraordinary beauty. Sukhothai is the birthplace of the festival and retains its spiritual character better than the tourist-heavy Chiang Mai version. The Historical Park becomes a floating garden of light.
travelNotes: Sukhothai is 7 hours from Bangkok by bus or 40 minutes from Phitsanulok (with airport) by car. The Historical Park is the main venue; buy a combined festival/park ticket. Loy Krathong falls on the full moon of the 12th Thai lunar month — approximately November. Accommodation in Sukhothai town is limited; book early. Celebrations happen for about two weeks, culminating on Mardi Gras (French for "Fat Tuesday"), a celebratory name for Shrove Tuesday, the day before Ash Wednesday, which is the start of Lent in the Western Christian tradition. The overall season is known as Carnival and begins on 12th Night, January 6th, and extends until midnight before Ash Wednesday. Parties known as Club or Krewe balls start soon after, many of which are private, with their Kings and Queens coming from wealthy old families and their courts consisting of the season's debutantes. Most of the high society Krewes do not stage parades. As Fat Tuesday approaches, Mardi Gras parades start in earnest. name: Maslenitsa
bestFor: Winter travel; Food travel; Traditional heritage
description: Maslenitsa (Belarusian: Масленіца; Russian: Мaсленица [ˈmas⁽ʲ⁾lʲɪnʲɪt͡sə]; Rusyn: Пущаня; Ukrainian: Масниця), also known as Butter Lady, Butter Week, Crepe week, or Cheesefare Week, is an Eastern Slavic religious and folk holiday which has retained a number of elements of Slavic mythology in its ritual. It is celebrated during the last week before Great Lent; that is, the eighth week before Eastern Orthodox Pascha, equivalent to the West's Sexagesima. The date of Maslenitsa changes every year, depending on the date of the celebration of Easter. It corresponds to the Western Christian Carnival, except that Orthodox Lent begins on a Monday instead of a Wednesday, and the Orthodox date of Easter can differ greatly from the Western Christian date. The traditional attributes of the Maslenitsa celebration are the Maslenitsa effigy, sleigh rides, and festivities.…

name: Mosta Assumption Feast
description: The Assumption of Mary is a Catholic dogma that Mary, at the end of her earthly life, was taken body and soul into heaven, defined by Pope Pius XII on 1 November 1950 in his apostolic constitution Munificentissimus Deus. It is celebrated on 15 August. It leaves open the question of whether Mary died or whether she was raised to eternal life without bodily death. The equivalent belief in the Eastern Christianity is the Dormition of the Mother of God or the "Falling Asleep of the Mother of God". In the Lutheran Churches, 15 August is celebrated as the Feast of St. Mary. A number of Anglican denominations observe 15 August under various titles, including the Feast of Saint Mary the Virgin or the Falling Asleep of the Blessed Virgin Mary. The word 'assumption' derives from the Latin word assūmptiō, meaning 'taking up'.

name: Mysuru Dasara
description: Mysuru Dasara is arguably India's most regal festival — a 10-day celebration of the goddess Chamundeshwari that culminates in the Vijayadashami procession. The Mysore Palace is illuminated with 100,000 lights every evening throughout the festival. The final day procession features a golden howdah (palanquin) mounted on a decorated elephant carrying the idol of the goddess, accompanied by tableaux, music, and thousands of participants in royal dress. The Maharaja of Mysore has sponsored this festival since the 14th century.

name: Naadam Festival
description: Naadam is Mongolia's national festival — a three-day celebration of the three 'manly sports': wrestling, archery, and horse racing. The horse racing takes place 40km outside the city, with child jockeys (ages 5–13) riding for up to 30km across open steppe; the wrestlers compete in the National Stadium in elaborate ceremonial costumes. The combination of thundering horses, half-dressed wrestlers performing ritual moves, and archery in traditional del robes makes Naadam a window into a civilisation that has changed surprisingly little since the time of Genghis Khan. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.
travelNotes: The opening ceremony at the National Stadium on July 11 is the most important event — tickets must be booked well ahead. name: Nada no Kenka Matsuri
description: Nada no Kenka Matsuri (Fighting Festival) is one of Japan's most ferociously physical festivals. Three portable shrines (mikoshi) are deliberately crashed into each other repeatedly until one is destroyed, while teams of hundreds of men in traditional dress compete to control enormous decorative floats (yatai). The violence is ritual but real — the goal is to create chaos as an offering to the gods. Held at Matsubara Shrine in Himeji, it has been running for over 360 years.
travelNotes: Himeji is on the Sanyo Shinkansen line, about 30 minutes from Kobe. The festival runs from dawn to dusk over two days; the main clashes happen in the late afternoon. Standing room only for the best views — arrive early. October in Himeji is mild and pleasant.

Nadur is a small locality with a population of 4500, on the island of Gozo - the sister island of Malta - in the middle of the Mediterranean about 50 km south of Sicily. It is run by a Local Council with 5 elected members: the Mayor, Deputy Mayor and 3 Councilors with one full-time Executive Secretary. Besides the religious feast of St. Peter & St. Paul, known locally as "L-Imnarja" held on 29 June and which lasts a whole week, Nadur is also famous for its annual revelry held on the 5 days preceding Ash Wednesday, the beginning of Lent.…

name: Naga Fireballs Festival
description: On the last night of Buddhist Lent (Ok Phansa), hundreds of glowing orbs rise silently from the Mekong River at Nong Khai — the Naga Fireballs (Bung Fai Phaya Nark). The phenomenon has been documented for centuries and is attributed by locals to the Naga (mythical serpent king) breathing fire from the river bottom. Crowds of Thais and a small number of foreign visitors line the riverbank to watch the fireballs rise and dissolve in the night air. The scientific explanation is debated; the experience is undeniable.

name: Navruz in Samarkand
bestFor: Cultural immersion; Traditional heritage; Food travel
description: Navruz (Persian New Year) on March 21 is the most important holiday across Central Asia and Iran — a pre-Islamic spring festival that predates Islam by over 3,000 years. Samarkand's version, held amid the extraordinary Timurid architecture of Registan Square, is the most visually spectacular. Sumalak (a wheat-germ pudding) is prepared communally over several days, traditional games (including ulak tartysh, a polo-like game with a headless goat carcass) are played, and music, dance, and enormous communal feasts fill the streets.
travelNotes: March 21 is the fixed date. Samarkand is accessible by high-speed rail from Tashkent (2 hours) or Bukhara (1.5 hours). Spring in Samarkand is ideal — warm days, cool nights, and the apricot trees in bloom. Uzbekistan's e-visa system makes entry straightforward. The Registan Square setting makes this one of the most architecturally spectacular festival locations in the world.

The Notte della Taranta is focused on pizzica, a popular folk genre in Salento, and takes place in various municipalities in the province of Lecce and the Grecìa Salentina, especially in Melpignano. It gives great importance to the folk music tradition of taranta and pizzica, and it is a resource for tourism in Apulia. The festival tours around Salento, normally culminating in a grand finale concert in Melpignano in August, which lasts until late night. An average of 120,000 spectators attend the last concert every year. The festival started in 1998 by an initiative of several municipalities of the Salento, which sponsored the event. Every year a new musical director is chosen. In 2015, Ludovico Einaudi released his album Taranta Project, inspired by his time directing the festival.

name: Nowruz in Isfahan

During Nowruz, the square fills with families picnicking, horse-drawn carriages circling, and the enormous fire-jumping (Chaharshanbe Suri) bonfires lit in the streets of the old city the evening before the new year.

name: Nyepi (Day of Silence)
bestFor: Cultural immersion; Traditional heritage; Local authenticity
description: Nyepi is the Balinese Hindu New Year — and the only public holiday on earth that requires total silence. For 24 hours, the entire island of Bali shuts down: no lights, no fires, no work, no travel, no noise. The airport closes. Streets are empty. Tourists must remain in their hotels. The day before, enormous papier-mâché demon effigies (ogoh-ogoh) are paraded through every village to drive away evil spirits, then burned. The contrast between the wild ogoh-ogoh procession and the following day of complete stillness is extraordinary.
travelNotes: The ogoh-ogoh parade the evening before Nyepi (Pengerupukan) is the unmissable visual spectacle — position yourself in Ubud or Denpasar by late afternoon. On Nyepi itself, hotels remain open with candlelit dinners and activities but you cannot leave. Embrace it — the island's enforced silence is genuinely powerful. Bali's airport is closed for 24 hours; don't book flights on Nyepi day.

name: Obby Oss Festival

It involves two separate processions making their way around the town, each containing an eponymous hobby horse known as the 'Obby 'Oss. The festival starts at midnight on the 30th of April when townspeople gather outside the Golden Lion Inn to sing the "Night Song". By morning, the town has been dressed with greenery and flowers placed around the maypole. The excitement begins with the appearance of one of the 'Obby 'Osses. Male dancers cavort through the town dressed as one of two 'Obby 'Osses, the "Old" and the "Blue Ribbon" or "Methodist" 'Obby 'Osses; as the name suggests, they are stylised depictions of horses. Prodded on by assistants known as "Teasers", each wears a mask and black frame-hung cape under which they try to catch young maidens as they pass through the town.…

It combines a beer festival with a fun fair and is held annually in Munich on the Theresienwiese from mid-September to the first Sunday in October. The event draws around seven million visitors each year. In 2023, attendance reached a record 7.2 million. Visitors consumed approximately 7.4 million litres of beer. The festival features amusement rides, games, food stalls, and traditional Bavarian dishes. The first Oktoberfest was held on 12 October 1810 to celebrate the wedding of Crown Prince Ludwig and Princess Therese of Saxony-Hildburghausen. Since then, the festival has been cancelled on multiple occasions, most recently in 2020 and 2021 during the COVID-19 pandemic in Germany.

name: Ommegang — Brussels
description: The Ommegang of Brussels (French: Ommegang de Bruxelles; Dutch: Ommegang van Brussel) is a traditional Ommegang, a type of medieval pageant, held annually in Brussels, Belgium. It is celebrated for its elaborate display and deep cultural significance. Originating in the 14th century as Brussels' largest lustral procession honouring a miraculous statue of the Virgin Mary, the event gradually evolved into a civic spectacle involving all the city's constituents. Since 1930, it has taken the form of a historical reenactment of the Joyous Entry of Emperor Charles V and his son, Philip II, in the city in 1549. The colourful parade includes floats, traditional processional giants, and scores of folkloric groups, either on foot or on horseback, dressed in medieval garb. As such, it joins the wider tradition of processions of notable riders and giants found throughout Belgium and northern France.…

name: Onam Festival
bestFor: Cultural immersion; Traditional heritage; Food travel
description: Onam is Kerala's most important harvest festival — a 10-day celebration of the mythical King Mahabali's annual return to his kingdom. The festival features the Vallamkali snake boat races on the Pampa river, spectacular pookalam floral carpets laid outside every home, Thiruvathirakali dance, and the elaborate Onasadya feast of 26 dishes served on banana leaves. The Thrissur Pooram (held earlier in the year) is often confused with Onam but both represent Kerala's extraordinary festival culture.
travelNotes: Onam dates shift annually with the Malayalam calendar — verify the Thiruvonam date for 2026. Kerala in late August/early September is post-monsoon; pleasant and green. The Aranmula boat race and Nehru Trophy boat race are the most famous snake boat events — book grandstand tickets early. Kochi or Thiruvananthapuram are good bases with good transport connections.

name: Onbashira Festival

description: Onbashira (the Honored Pillars) is held every six years at the four Suwa Grand Shrines in Nagano Prefecture. Men ride enormous fir logs — up to 17 metres long and weighing 10 tonnes — down steep mountain slopes in a ritual called Yamadashi. Falls and injuries are common; deaths have occurred and are considered honourable. The logs are then hauled upright to mark the corners of the shrine buildings. Next occurrence: April–May 2028. One of the oldest documented festivals in Japan, traced back 1,200 years.
travelNotes: The next Onbashira is 2028 — worth planning far in advance. The Yamadashi (log descent) phase in April is the most dramatic. Suwa is about 2.5 hours from Tokyo by limited express train. Viewing spots fill by dawn on event days. The scale and danger make this unlike any other festival in Japan.

name: Otaru Snow Light Path Festival
description: The Otaru Snow Light Path Festival (Yuki Akari no Michi) lines the city's famous canal and side streets with thousands of hand-made snow lanterns and candles. Unlike Sapporo's grand ice sculptures, Otaru's festival is intimate — glowing lanterns reflected in the canal, jazz drifting from nearby bars, steam from hot drinks. The combination of Meiji-era stone warehouses, candlelit snow, and the quiet canal makes it one of Japan's most photogenic winter events. Often combined with a day trip from Sapporo (40 min by train).
travelNotes: Best visited in the evening from 5pm when lanterns are lit. Combine with Sapporo Snow Festival for a full Hokkaido winter trip. Otaru canal area is walkable from the station. name: Ouidah Voodoo Festival
description: The Ouidah Voodoo Festival on January 10 is the annual national celebration of Voodoo in Benin — the country where the religion originated before being exported to the Americas through the slave trade. Priests and devotees from across Benin and West Africa gather on Ouidah's beach for ceremonies, animal sacrifices, possession rituals, and the veneration of Voodoo deities (vodun). The Point of No Return — the arch through which enslaved people passed onto ships — is the central monument. Deeply serious and spiritually powerful.
travelNotes: Ouidah is 40km from Cotonou. January in coastal Benin is hot and humid. The festival is free and open but requires respectful, quiet observation. Photography near active ceremonies requires explicit permission. Benin is one of West Africa's more politically stable countries; standard travel precautions apply.

name: Pacu Jawi Bull Racing
description: Pacu Jawi is a bull racing tradition in West Sumatra's Tanah Datar Regency — but unlike any other bull racing in the world. A jockey holds the tails of two bulls simultaneously as they charge across a flooded rice field; the mud sprays, the bulls lunge, and the jockey tries to remain standing while steering with only tail tension. The result is a genuinely chaotic, high-speed spectacle that produces extraordinary muddy photography. Held after the rice harvest across rotating villages in the regency from approximately August to November.
travelNotes: Pacu Jawi is held across rotating villages in Tanah Datar Regency; check the specific schedule through local tourism offices. Padang (West Sumatra's capital) is the access city — 90 minutes from the regency by road. Bring a waterproof camera cover — you will be within mud-spray range. The event is entirely local; guides from Padang can arrange transportation.

name: Pahiyas Festival
description: Pahiyas is the Philippines' most colourful single-day festival — the entire town of Lucban in Quezon Province covers its houses in kiping (translucent coloured rice wafers), vegetables, fruits, and harvested crops in a competition for the most elaborate decoration. Every facade becomes an installation artwork; windows framed in giant kiping flowers, doorways hung with pineapples and papayas. The festival honours San Isidro Labrador (patron of farmers) and takes place on May 15. The narrow streets are packed but entirely walkable.
travelNotes: Lucban is 130km from Manila — about 3 hours by bus via Lucena. May 15 is the fixed date. Arrive early (by 7am) before the streets fill. The judging of house decorations happens in the morning; houses are then stripped after the procession. The town is small and the festival is genuinely community-made — no corporate sponsorship. Lucban longganisa sausage is famous; eat at any local stall.

name: Paleni Carodejnic Prague

This feast commemorates the canonization of Saint Walpurga and the movement of her relics to Eichstätt, both of which occurred on 1 May 870. Saint Walpurga was hailed by the Christians of Germany for battling "pest, rabies, and whooping cough, as well as against witchcraft". Christians prayed to God through the intercession of Saint Walpurga in order to protect themselves from witchcraft, as Saint Walpurga was successful in converting the local populace to Christianity.…

Ten horses and riders, bareback and dressed in the appropriate colours, represent ten of the seventeen contrade, or city wards, in a tradition dating back to the 17th century. The Palio held on 2 July is named Palio di Provenzano, in honour of the Madonna of Provenzano, a Marian devotion particular to Siena which developed around an icon from the Terzo Camollia area of the city. The Palio held on 16 August is named Palio dell'Assunta, in honour of the Assumption of Mary.…

name: Paro Tsechu Festival
description: Paro Tsechu is the most famous of Bhutan's many Tsechu festivals — a five-day Buddhist festival at Paro Dzong (fortress-monastery) where monks and laypeople perform sacred cham masked dances in elaborate silk costumes. The final morning features the Thongdrel — an enormous silk appliqué thangka (religious painting) the size of a building facade, unfurled before dawn and rolled up before the sun touches it, believed to grant liberation to all who witness it. The setting of a white-washed dzong against forested mountains makes Bhutan's tsechus visually unmatched.
travelNotes: Bhutan requires all foreign visitors to book through a licensed Bhutanese tour operator and pay a Sustainable Development Fee (currently $100/day). Carnival typically involves public celebrations, including events such as parades, public street parties and other entertainments, combining some elements of a circus. Elaborate costumes and masks allow people to set aside their everyday individuality and experience a heightened sense of social unity. Participants often indulge in excessive consumption of alcohol, meat, and other foods that will be forgone during upcoming Lent. Traditionally, butter, milk, and other animal products were not consumed "excessively", rather, their stock was fully consumed during Shrovetide as to reduce waste.…

name: Patum de Berga
description: The Patum de Berga (Catalan pronunciation: [pəˈtum də ˈβeɾɣə]), or simply La Patum, is a popular and traditional festival that is celebrated each year in the Catalan city of Berga (Barcelona) during Corpus Christi. It consists of a series of "dances" (called Salts) by townspeople carrying mystical and symbolic figures and accompanied either by the rhythm of a drum—the tabal, whose sound gives the festival its name—or band music. The salts are marked by their solemnity and their ample use of fire and pyrotechnics. It was declared a Traditional Festival of National Interest by the Generalitat de Catalunya in 1983. In 2005, UNESCO declared it one of the Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity and, in 2008, inscribed it on its Representative List.

name: Phi Ta Khon Ghost Festival
description: Phi Ta Khon (Ghost Festival) in the tiny town of Dan Sai in Loei Province is one of Thailand's most peculiar and wonderful festivals. Locals dress as spirits in enormous handmade masks (made from bamboo steamer lids and coconut husks) and brightly coloured patchwork costumes, parading through town making mischief, dancing, and performing mock rituals. The festival is based on a Buddhist legend and is entirely community-made — no commercial costume hire, every mask unique. Profoundly local and barely covered in mainstream travel media.
travelNotes: Dan Sai is 330km from Bangkok — best reached by overnight bus or private car via Phitsanulok. The town has very limited accommodation; book months ahead or stay in Loei town (50km away). name: Phuket Vegetarian Festival
description: Phuket's Vegetarian Festival (Nine Emperor Gods Festival) is one of Southeast Asia's most extreme religious spectacles. Chinese Taoist devotees observe strict vegetarianism for nine days while spirit mediums (mah song) enter trance states and pierce their cheeks, tongues, and bodies with skewers, swords, and unlikely objects without apparent pain. The street processions of pierced devotees — surrounded by firecrackers, incense, and white-clad devotees — are entirely sincere religious rituals, not performances. The most intense version of this festival in Thailand.
travelNotes: October follows the lunar 9th month calendar — verify 2026 dates. Phuket Old Town is the festival centre; the Chinese temples on Thalang Road are the main sites. Photography requires sensitivity near pierced devotees — ask with gestures for permission. The firecrackers are extremely loud and thrown at close range; ear protection recommended. The festival runs 9 days with the most intense processions on days 1, 5, and 9.

name: Pierogi Festival
description: Pierogi ( pirr-OH-ghee; Polish: [pjɛˈrɔɡʲi] , sg. pieróg [ˈpjɛruk] ) are filled dumplings made by wrapping unleavened dough around a filling and cooking in boiling water. They are occasionally flavored with a savory or sweet garnish. Typical fillings include potato and twaróg cheese, sauerkraut, ground meat, mushrooms, fruits, or berries. Savory pierogi are often served with a topping of sour cream, fried onions, or both. Dumplings under the name pierogi are a traditional Polish dish, holding considerable culinary significance in Poland. The recipe itself dates back to at least 1682, when Poland's first cookbook, Compendium ferculorum, albo Zebranie potraw, was published. Pierogi are also popular in modern-day Canadian and American cuisines.

name: Pingxi Sky Lantern Festival
description: The Pingxi Sky Lantern Festival (Chinese: 平溪天燈節; Tongyong Pinyin: Píngsī Tiāndēng Jié; Tâi-lô: Pîng-khe thian-ting-tsiat) is an annual festival held in Pingxi District of New Taipei City, Taiwan, during the Taiwan Lantern Festival, which marks the end of the Lunar New Year celebrations. The event is known for the release of large numbers of sky lanterns into the night sky, often bearing written wishes or prayers. Originally a local custom, the festival gained wider recognition around the turn of the 21st century and now attracts visitors from across Taiwan and abroad.

name: Pingxi Sky Lantern Festival
description: The Pingxi Sky Lantern Festival on the full moon of the first lunar month (Lantern Festival) is Taiwan's most iconic image: thousands of paper sky lanterns rising simultaneously from the narrow mountain valley town of Pingxi, wishes written in red on their sides, glowing against the dark sky. The effect of hundreds of lanterns ascending together, reflecting in the Jilong River, is genuinely moving. Multiple release events happen throughout the day and evening; the official midnight release coordinated by Taiwan's Tourism Bureau is the most dramatic.

name: Pohoda Festival

name: Sânziene Festival

description: Sânziană is the Romanian name for gentle fairies who play an important part in local folklore, also used to designate the Galium verum or Cruciata laevipes flowers. Under the plural form Sânziene, the word designates an annual festival in the fairies' honor. Etymologically, the name comes from the Latin Sancta Diana, the Roman goddess of the hunt and moon, also celebrated in Roman Dacia (ancient Romania). Diana was known to be the virgin goddess and looked after virgins and women. She was one of the three maiden goddesses, Diana, Minerva and Vesta, who swore never to marry. People in the western Carpathian Mountains celebrate the Sânziene holiday annually, on June 24. This is similar to the Swedish Midsummer holiday, and is believed to be a pagan celebration of the summer solstice in June.…

name: Sinulog Festival
description: Sinulog is the Philippines' most spectacular street festival — a Grand Parade of elaborately costumed contingents from across the country dancing the two-steps-forward, one-step-back Sinulog rhythm through the streets of Cebu City in honour of the Santo Niño. The costumes are on a par with the Carnival of Venice in ambition and visual impact. Over a million people attend the Sunday Grand Parade. The festival is rooted in the arrival of Magellan and the baptism of Cebu's queen in 1521 — the Santo Niño figurine given then is still venerated today.
travelNotes: The Grand Parade is on the third Sunday of January — book accommodation 3–4 months ahead as Cebu fills completely. Cebu has an international airport. name: Songkran
description: Thai New Year, also known as Songkran (Thai: สงกรานต์, pronounced [sǒŋ.krāːn]), or the Songkran Festival (Songkran Splendours), is the Thai New Year's national holiday. It is celebrated annually on 13 April, with the holiday period extending from 14 to 15 April. The festival aligns with New Year celebrations observed in many South and Southeast Asian cultures, following the Theravada Buddhist calendar, and coincides with related observances in the Hindu calendar. In Thailand, the New Year is officially observed on 1 January. Songkran was the official New Year until 1888, when it was changed to 1 April, before being shifted to 1 January in 1940. The traditional Thai New Year, Songkran, remains a national holiday. Celebrations are known for public water fights, which symbolize ritual cleansing. The festival has become popular among international visitors and contributes significantly to tourism.

The name midsummer mainly refers to summer solstice festivals of European origin. These cultures traditionally regard it as the middle of summer, with the season beginning on May Day. name: Thaipusam
description: Thaipusam in Penang is one of the most intense religious festivals on earth. Tamil Hindu devotees carry kavadi — elaborate steel frames pierced through their skin, cheeks, and tongue — in fulfilment of vows to Lord Murugan. The procession from Sri Mahamariamman Temple to Arulmigu Balathandayuthapani Temple on Penang Hill is accompanied by drumming, chanting, and enormous crowds. The devotees enter a trance state and appear to feel no pain. Penang's version is particularly concentrated and accessible compared to Kuala Lumpur's Batu Caves version.
travelNotes: Thaipusam date follows the Tamil lunar calendar — the January/February full moon. Penang's festival is more intimate and accessible than KL. Wear conservative clothing near the temples. Photography is permitted but be respectful — ask before photographing devotees with kavadi. George Town has excellent accommodation and food at all price points.

name: Thingyan Water Festival
bestFor: Adrenaline seekers; Cultural immersion; Local authenticity
description: Thingyan is the Myanmar New Year water festival — a four-day celebration during which the entire country erupts in water throwing. Unlike Thailand's Songkran (which Thingyan predates and inspired), the Mandalay version includes elaborate temporary stages (pandals) with performers, traditional dance, and social commentary. Water is thrown by everyone at everyone — on the streets, from rooftops, through car windows. The symbolism is purification: washing away the sins of the old year. Myanmar's recent political situation means visitor numbers have dropped; check travel advisories before planning.
travelNotes: Check Myanmar travel advisories carefully before planning — the political situation has been unstable since 2021. April is the hottest time of year in Myanmar (40°C+); wear lightweight quick-dry clothing. Everything electronic needs waterproofing. Mandalay is the cultural capital and the best city for Thingyan; Yangon's version is larger but less traditionally rooted.

name: Thrissur Pooram
description: Thrissur Pooram is Kerala's most spectacular temple festival — a single-day event that packs more visual intensity than almost any other festival in India. Two processions of caparisoned elephants (15 on each side) face each other in the temple grounds while teams compete in elaborate parasol-exchange rituals and a thunderous firework display begins before dawn. The fireworks at Thrissur Pooram are not the usual finale — they run from 3am to 7am and are competed between the two participating temples for supremacy. The crowd numbers in the hundreds of thousands.
travelNotes: This is a one-day event — plan precisely. The pre-dawn fireworks start around 3am; most visitors stay up through the night. May in Thrissur is extremely hot and humid — hydrate aggressively. Thrissur is well connected by train from Kochi (1 hour) and Kozhikode. The Thekkinkadu Maidan (the festival ground) fills completely by morning.

name: Tihar Festival of Lights
description: Tihar (Deepawali in Nepal) is Nepal's five-day festival of lights — each day dedicated to a different creature or deity: crows, dogs, cows, oxen, and finally the self (Bhai Tika). Dogs are garlanded with marigolds and given tikka marks on their foreheads on the second day — an extraordinary sight that reflects Hinduism's reverence for the animal world. On the third day (Laxmi Puja), Kathmandu and the Kathmandu Valley illuminate completely with oil lamps, candles, and electric lights. The Newari community celebrates with a separate night festival called Mha Puja.

The Laxmi Puja evening (third night) is the main visual event — the valley fills with light. The dog-worship day (Kukur Tihar) produces some of the most heartwarming photography of any festival in Asia.

name: Tiji Festival

The dances enact the defeat of a demon by the deity Dorje Jono and are performed with enormous silk costumes, elaborate masks, and ritual implements against the backdrop of the ancient walled city of Lo Manthang — the last intact medieval walled city in the Himalayas. Until 1992, Upper Mustang was closed entirely to outsiders.

A limited number of visitors are permitted; the festival remains genuinely remote and exclusive.

name: Timkat (Epiphany)

Gondar's celebration, held among the royal castle complex (Fasil Ghebbi), involves priests carrying the tabot (replica of the Ark of the Covenant) through the streets in procession to a pool of water, where it is blessed and used to symbolically baptise the faithful. The colour of the priestly vestments, the umbrellas (ceremonial parasols), the drums, and the chanting in Ge'ez (Ethiopia's ancient liturgical language) create an unforgettable experience.

Gondar has an airport with connections from Addis Ababa (1.5 hours). January in Gondar is dry and warm. Accommodation books up months ahead for Timkat. name: Transhumance Provence
description: Transhumance is a type of pastoralism or nomadism, a seasonal movement of livestock between fixed summer and winter pastures. In montane regions (vertical transhumance), it implies movement between higher pastures in summer and lower valleys in winter. Herders have a permanent home, typically in valleys. Generally only the herds travel, with a certain number of people necessary to tend them, while the main population stays at the base. In contrast, movement in plains or plateaus (horizontal transhumance) is more susceptible to disruption by climatic, economic, or political change. Traditional or fixed transhumance has occurred throughout the inhabited world, particularly Europe and western Asia. It is often important to pastoralist societies, as the dairy products of transhumance flocks and herds (milk, butter, yogurt and cheese) may form much of the diet of such populations.…
